summary: African-American Nobel Prize-winning author (1993), known for novels exploring Black American experience. Editor at Random House who diversified publishing. Associated with Ohio settings and themes of race, memory, and trauma.

works.name: Beloved
works.description: Morrison's most celebrated novel, dedicated to 'Sixty Million and more,' inspired by the true story of Margaret Garner. Set at 124 Bluestone Road, the escaped slave Sethe is haunted by the ghost of the daughter she killed to save from slavery. Paul D arrives from the Sweet Home plantation, where they were both enslaved under Schoolteacher. Sethe's back bears scars she describes as a 'chokecherry tree.' The ghost Beloved materializes as a young woman. Denver, Sethe's surviving daughter, eventually seeks help from the community. Key moments include Sethe attacking Mr. Bodwin with an ice pick (mistaking him for Schoolteacher), Sixo being burned alive, and the famous opening line '124 was spiteful.'

works.name: Song of Solomon
works.description: The novel follows Milkman Dead III, named after the janitor Freddie catches Ruth breastfeeding him well into childhood. His sisters First Corinthians and Magdalene Called Lena were named by randomly putting a pin in the Bible. The novel opens with Robert Smith jumping from the roof of Mercy Hospital. Milkman discovers the Seven Days, a vengeful society, and his friend Guitar Bains is a member. He travels to Shalimar, Virginia, to learn his family history and discovers his grandfather Solomon could fly. The theme of flight permeates the novel, from the opening song 'O Sugarman' to Milkman leaping toward Guitar at the end. His aunt Pilate has no navel.

works.name: The Bluest Eye
works.description: Morrison's first novel (1970), set in Lorain, Ohio. Pecola Breedlove, an African-American girl, desires blue eyes as a symbol of white beauty, inspired by a Shirley Temple mug. She is raped by her alcoholic father Cholly, and the resulting premature baby dies — foreshadowed by the failure of marigolds to bloom. The novel's sections are titled after seasons, and its chapter headings parody Dick and Jane primers, with punctuation and then spaces progressively removed. The charlatan Soaphead Church tricks Pecola into poisoning a dog named Bob to 'grant' her the title feature. Three prostitutes — China, Poland, and Miss Marie — live above the Breedlove storefront apartment. Claudia MacTeer narrates and, with her sister Frieda, plants marigold seeds in hopes that Pecola's baby will live; Claudia also dismembers a white doll resembling Shirley Temple. Pecola is blamed for killing Geraldine's cat. The novel also recounts the abusive pasts of Pecola's parents Cholly and Pauline ('Polly'), and a scene in which Polly screams during childbirth to 'let them people know having a baby was more than a bowel movement.'

works.name: Jazz
works.description: The second novel in Morrison's Beloved trilogy. Joe Trace, a cosmetics salesman, has an ill-fated affair with the young Dorcas and murders her. Joe's wife Violet, nicknamed 'Violent,' stabs Dorcas's corpse at the funeral. Joe traces his heritage to a homeless woman named Wild. The novel is set in Harlem.

works.name: Paradise
works.description: The third novel in Morrison's trilogy. Opens with the line 'They shoot the white girl first,' but never reveals which Convent resident is white. Nine men from the all-Black town of Ruby, Oklahoma attack the women at the Convent. The town was founded by darker-skinned Black residents fleeing colorism in their former home of Haven. The Oven, a communal structure, has a disputed inscription about 'the Furrow of His Brow.' Midwife Lone DuPres is shunned for allegedly causing birth defects.

works.name: Tar Baby
works.description: Centers on the affair between the stowaway Son and the Sorbonne-educated Jadine Childs on the Caribbean Isle des Chevaliers, where the wealthy Valerian Street has retired. Valerian's wife Margaret abused their son Michael. The novel ends with Son running into the forest to join blind, naked horsemen. Morrison said the title 'came to mean the black woman who can hold things together.'

works.name: Sula
works.description: Set in the neighborhood called the Bottom, where the PTSD-afflicted veteran Shadrack leads National Suicide Day on January 3rd each year. The title character Sula sleeps with her best friend Nel's husband and accidentally kills the boy Chicken Little. Sula's grandmother Eva allegedly lost her leg by placing it on a railroad track for insurance money.

clues.clue: National Suicide Day on January 3rd; veteran Shadrack; set in the Bottom

works.name: A Mercy
works.description: Set in 17th-century Virginia on the estate of the rum trader Jacob Vaark. Characters include the enslaved Angolan Florens, the Native American servant Lina, and Rebekah Vaark. Both Florens and Lina survive a smallpox epidemic. The narrative is written with a nail onto the floor of a mansion.

clues.clue: Set in 17th-century Virginia; Jacob Vaark's estate; Florens, Lina, Rebekah
examples: the slave Florens, the Native American laborer Lina, and Rebekah Vaark, the inhabitants of a seventeenth-century Virginia farm

works.name: Playing in the Dark
works.description: Morrison's book of literary criticism adapted from her 1990 Massey Lectures. She explores the 'Africanist presence' in American literature and asserts the importance of 'American Africanism' to the American literary tradition. The chapter 'Romancing the Shadow' analyzes Poe's Arthur Gordon Pym and the ending's 'figurations of impenetrable whiteness.' She also discusses Willa Cather's Sapphira and the Slave Girl.

clues.clue: Explores 'Africanist presence' in American literature; adapted from 1990 Massey Lectures
examples: adapted her lectures on the whiteness of American literature into Playing in the Dark
explores an 'Africanist presence' central to American literature

clues.clue: 'Romancing the Shadow' chapter analyzes Poe's Arthur Gordon Pym
examples: 'Romancing the Shadow' begins with an analysis of the 'white ghost' that appears at the end of this adventure novel by Edgar Allan Poe

works.name: Recitatif
works.description: Morrison's only short story. State wards Roberta and Twyla switch beds every night while living at a shelter and meet repeatedly throughout their lives. The story never reveals which character is Black and which is white. They fight by raising protest signs, and one questions whether the mute cook Maggie was really Black.

clues.clue: Morrison's only short story; Roberta and Twyla; racial ambiguity; mute cook Maggie
examples: State wards Roberta and Twyla switch beds every night while living at a shelter
this author's only short story

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Morrison was the first Black woman to win the Nobel Prize in Literature (1993). She was an editor at Random House, where she compiled The Black Book and diversified publishing. She was born Chloe Ardelia Wofford. She won the 1988 Pulitzer Prize after dozens of writers petitioned for it. Her works are often set in Ohio (Lorain, specifically). She collaborated on operas including Desdemona (with Peter Sellars) and Margaret Garner (with Richard Danielpour). Her acceptance speech included the parable of the blind old woman and the bird that represents language.

clues.clue: Nobel Prize in Literature (1993); first Black woman Nobel laureate in literature
examples: this 1993 Nobel Laureate
this Black Nobel laureate

clues.clue: Editor at Random House; compiled The Black Book
examples: acquisitions editor with diversifying publishing in the 1970s at Random House
compiled The Black Book while at Random House

clues.clue: Collaborated on operas Desdemona (with Peter Sellars) and Margaret Garner (Danielpour)
examples: collaborated with Peter Sellars and Rokia Traore on the 2012 opera Desdemona

clues.clue: Nobel speech: parable of blind old woman, bird representing language
examples: a parable imagines young people trying to trick a blind old woman by asking if a bird representing language is alive or dead

comprehensive_summary:
Toni Morrison is among the most heavily clued American authors in quizbowl, recognized as the first Black woman to win the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993. Born Chloe Ardelia Wofford, she served as an editor at Random House where she compiled The Black Book and championed Black authors. Her works are frequently set in Ohio and the American South, and she is known for her rich exploration of African-American history, memory, and identity.

Beloved is Morrison's most prominent work in clues, set at 124 Bluestone Road where the escaped slave Sethe is haunted by the ghost of the daughter she killed to prevent her return to slavery. The novel is dedicated to 'Sixty Million and more' and was inspired by the real story of Margaret Garner. Key details include Sethe's back scars described as a 'chokecherry tree,' Paul D's arrival from the Sweet Home plantation, Schoolteacher's cruelty (including burning Sixo alive), and the famous opening '124 was spiteful.'

Song of Solomon follows Milkman Dead III, named because Freddie caught Ruth breastfeeding him well into childhood. The theme of flight pervades the novel from the opening (Robert Smith jumping from Mercy Hospital's roof and the song 'O Sugarman') to Milkman's discovery that his ancestor Solomon could fly. The Seven Days vigilante society and Guitar Bains are frequently clued, as is Milkman's journey to Shalimar, Virginia.

The Bluest Eye, Morrison's 1970 debut set in Lorain, Ohio, depicts Pecola Breedlove's tragic desire for blue eyes as a symbol of white beauty — inspired by a Shirley Temple mug. Pecola is raped by her alcoholic father Cholly, her premature baby dies, and the failure of marigolds to bloom foreshadows this. The novel's chapter headings parody Dick and Jane primers, with punctuation and then spaces progressively removed; its sections are titled after seasons. The charlatan Soaphead Church tricks Pecola into poisoning a dog named Bob to 'give' her blue eyes. Three prostitutes — China, Poland, and Miss Marie — live above the Breedloves' storefront apartment. Claudia MacTeer narrates and plants marigold seeds with her sister Frieda; Claudia also dismembers a white doll resembling Shirley Temple. Pecola is blamed for killing Geraldine's cat, and flashbacks recount the abusive pasts of her parents Cholly and Pauline ('Polly'). Jazz and Paradise complete the Beloved trilogy: Jazz features Joe Trace's murder of Dorcas and Violet stabbing the corpse; Paradise opens 'They shoot the white girl first' and depicts nine men from Ruby, Oklahoma attacking women at the Convent.

Other works include Tar Baby (Son and Jadine on Isle des Chevaliers with Valerian Street), Sula (National Suicide Day, the Bottom, Shadrack), A Mercy (17th-century Virginia, Florens and Jacob Vaark), and her only short story 'Recitatif' (Roberta and Twyla's racially ambiguous friendship). Playing in the Dark, her literary criticism adapted from the Massey Lectures, examines the 'Africanist presence' in American literature. She also collaborated on operas and was known for her Nobel acceptance speech parable about a blind woman and a bird representing language.
